# Questions in Livestream 78

Live Stream #78: Lest We Forget

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=509" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:29</a> | How did he end up working with electronics, and did he need formal training? | He said it started as hobbyist tinkering and that he was essentially self-taught. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=666" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">11:06</a> | Bach or an Egyptian court musician? | He chose Bach, while noting how little ancient Egyptian music survives in recoverable form. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=769" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:49</a> | Could the Hyksos have used Israelites as soldiers against the late 17th Dynasty? | Yes, conceivably. He said descendants of Israelites could well have served in Hyksos forces. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=849" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">14:09</a> | What is wrong with dating most of the Torah to the first millennium BC while treating only the poetry as Mosaic? | He said that depends on a faulty assumption that poetry is earlier than prose, which ancient Near Eastern evidence does not support. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=1154" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:14</a> | Does the Bible rule out multiple timelines or universes? | No. He said nothing in Scripture clearly rules out a multiverse, and contingent-future texts may even gesture in that direction. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=1416" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:36</a> | Can we determine when John the Baptist was conceived from Zechariah's priestly division? | He was not prepared to say, because the second-temple priestly scheduling details are intricate and outside his strongest area. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=1591" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">26:31</a> | Where does he think Sharuhen was located, and why did Anson Rainey place it at Tell Haror rather than Tell el-Ajjul? | He did not know Rainey's reasoning and said the location remains difficult because Philistine divisions are still uncertain and written evidence is sparse. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=1756" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:16</a> | If he could lead an Egyptian field project with unlimited time and resources, where would he dig and what would he look for? | He named Saqqara for Imhotep's tomb and the outskirts of Avaris for Joseph's tomb, while noting many Egyptian sites would be worthwhile. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=2040" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:00</a> | What are his thoughts on Santa Claus, and is it good for Christian parents to keep that tradition? | He said Santa Claus can be fine if parents use it as a teaching moment about the historical Saint Nicholas and keep the gift-giving honest. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=2321" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">38:41</a> | Are Egyptian gods' public and private names related to Revelation 19:12? | He saw no direct connection; Egyptian private names relate to manipulating a god, while Revelation's hidden name points to something not yet revealed about Christ. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=2584" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">43:04</a> | Is Romans 1:16-20 referring to Second Temple myths of Abraham reasoning himself into monotheism from natural order? | He said the passage clearly appeals to general revelation, but he could not judge the Abraham-myth connection because he does not know those myths well enough. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=2831" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:11</a> | What does God do to unbelievers who sincerely do not believe or want to believe but cannot? | He said the fate of unbelievers is heavily debated, rejected universalism and annihilationism, and stressed that Scripture gives incomplete answers on some proposed alternatives. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=3237" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">53:57</a> | Were Jeremiah 46 and Ezekiel 29-30 failed prophecies because Babylon did not fully conquer, desolate, or scatter Egypt? | He said a Babylonian raid on Egypt is plausible and there may also be hyperbole, so he would not treat the passages simply as failed prophecies. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=3540" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">59:00</a> | Which older Pentateuch words were updated over time, like the word he had discussed for the Sea of Reeds? | He clarified that the example was likely `suf`, not Sabbath, and said its meaning shifted over time, though a Hebrew lexicographer could give fuller examples. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=3739" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:02:19</a> | Is the difference between First Kings 4:26 and Second Chronicles 9:25 over Solomon's stalls a scribal error or an idiom? | He said idiom is possible, especially for numbers like 40 and 12, but a scribal or harmonizing explanation is also possible and the evidence is not decisive. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=4122" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:42</a> | Can Samuel be treated as reliable history if it contains anachronisms, and what scholars are useful for studying it? | He recommended starting with NICOT and said some anachronisms are retrojections that do not automatically make a text historically unreliable. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=4544" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:15:44</a> | Is Psalm 2:12 messianic, and could `his wrath may soon be kindled` allude to a sudden coming? | He said the psalm is definitely messianic, while the exact force of `soon` or `in a little` remains somewhat interpretively open. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=4885" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:25</a> | Could Zechariah 14 predict the Maccabean revolt and end with Hanukkah? | He said Hanukkah could be a valid recontextualization of the passage, but Zechariah 14 is still about the day of the Lord and not necessarily its final fulfillment. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=5457" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:30:57</a> | Could Joshua's long day have been a Carrington-like event rather than a solar eclipse? | He thought a Carrington-like event is more reasonable than a solar eclipse, but emphasized that any explanation is speculative and not provable from the available data. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=5699" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:34:59</a> | Why was Israel not more powerful or majestic than other ancient Near Eastern nations, and could that be an objection to the faith? | He said Israel was meant to be holy rather than impressive, and that God using a weak nation displays God's strength rather than Israel's greatness. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=6046" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:40:46</a> | Could the Nuzi legal contracts be a dating factor for Genesis, or are they too late for the patriarchal narratives? | He had not read the Nuzi texts closely, but said they are not the only ancient legal contracts and would not necessarily limit Genesis to that period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=6347" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:47</a> | Do other religious texts receive as much rigorous historical scrutiny as the Bible? | He said no other religious text receives quite the same scrutiny, though texts making historical claims, such as the Quran or Mormon texts, do receive some. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=6715" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:55</a> | How many Jews were deported to Babylon, how many were left in Judah, and how many returned under Nehemiah? | He summarized scholarly estimates as roughly 10 percent deported and perhaps 20-30 percent of those returning, while noting uncertainty and the long survival of the Babylonian Jewish community. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=7125" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:58:45</a> | Would a movie about Judges in the style of a western be a good idea? | He was cautious about recontextualizing the Bible but said some setting shifts can communicate a story well, and Judges could have material that works that way. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=7418" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:03:38</a> | If he could invite any physicist onto the show, who would it be and why? | He said Schrodinger would be interesting if he were still alive, while noting he does not keep up with modern physicists. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=7592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:06:32</a> | Was Moses born in the late 18th Dynasty or the early 19th Dynasty? | He leaned to the late 18th Dynasty and warned that Moses's biblical age is expressed through repeated `40`-year idioms rather than a simple exact figure. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=8254" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:17:34</a> | What is death? | He defined death broadly as separation, reading Genesis 3 as a cascade of separations that Christ's work answers with restored life. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=8775" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:15</a> | How should someone respond when a friend rejects historical evidence by doubting all history? | He described this as cognitive dissonance and said there may be little constructive conversation at that point, apart from prayer and careful engagement. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=9318" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:35:18</a> | Has he read John Oswald's book `The Bible Among the Myths`, and what are his thoughts? | He had not read it and did not like calling the Bible myth, because most of the Bible does not fit that category in a classical sense. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=9562" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:39:22</a> | How does the gift of prophecy work, and are there modern prophets? | He believes spiritual gifts continue but said prophecy is rare, mostly forth-telling rather than foretelling, and must direct people back to God rather than to the prophet. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=10282" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:51:22</a> | Are phrases like `brothers and sisters` or `sons and daughters` separate words in Greek and Hebrew, or modern English interpretation? | He said it depends case by case; sometimes they are literal phrases and sometimes English translations expand a broader term such as `brothers`. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=10895" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:01:35</a> | How would he translate Genesis 1:1? | He prefers the traditional rendering, `In the beginning God created the heavens and the earth`, and wants translation kept distinct from later interpretation. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=11205" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:06:45</a> | Is Genesis 1 one sentence? | He said some modern translations make Genesis 1:1 and much of 1:2 a large subordinate clause, but he finds the traditional sentence structure more coherent. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=11608" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:13:28</a> | What are his thoughts on the Shroud of Turin? | He does not think it is Jesus's burial shroud, mainly because a real wrapped-body imprint should not look like a flat medieval-style image. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=11975" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:19:35</a> | What is the abbreviated process of making a new Bible translation, and would he want to make one? | He would not want to make one; most translations are committee revisions of earlier translations, and revision can produce better results than starting from scratch. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/SSu1vuv9p_0?t=12541" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:29:01</a> | Where does the term `Hebrew` come from in passages like First Samuel 29:3, and is it an insult there? | He said early uses likely relate to `hapiru` as a class term for outcasts or mercenaries, later shifting toward language and ethnic meanings. |
